preamble: L-vocalization is a widespread phonetic transformation where the alveolar lateral approximant /l/, particularly its velarized allophone [ɫ], is replaced by a vowel or a semivowel. This process frequently leads to the formation of diphthongs, especially when the vocalized /l/ becomes a back rounded vocoid (such as [u] or [w]) and subsequently merges with a preceding vowel to create an /ou/-like diphthong.

examples.note: The /l/ vocalizes to a /u/ or /w/ forming an /au/ diphthong.

sources: Recasens, D. (1996). An Articulatory-Perceptual Account of Vocalisation and Elision of Dark /l/ in the Romance Languages. Language and Speech, 39(1), 63-89.
L Vocalisation as a Natural Phenomenon - Essex Research Repository.
A phonetic interpretation of the sound changes affecting dark /l/ in Romance - ResearchGate.
